name: ask_user_for_clarification
description: While drafting the plan, surface ONE blocking question to the user only when an essential decision cannot be settled from context, recalled memory, or sensible defaults and the plan cannot be responsibly authored without it. In normal Plan Mode, prefer carrying open-but-nonblocking choices in the `clarifications` argument of the terminal `write_plan` call; those render inline in the Execute Plan popup and avoid stopping plan creation. Do NOT batch-call this tool in Plan Mode. Use it for: (1) missing information that fundamentally changes the plan shape; (2) ambiguous requirements with two or more materially different plan shapes; (3) risk confirmation for any destructive step in the plan (required, never assume). Do NOT use for: questions you can answer by reading code, calling `recall`, using sensible defaults, or recording an assumption in `write_plan`. CHOICE-ONLY UI: the user answers by clicking one of your `options` — there is NO free-text box, so always supply `options` and make every option concrete and directly selectable. NEVER offer placeholder options like "Provide stats", "Enter manually", "Skip", or "Other"; the user cannot type a value. Decompose open-ended quantities (weight, height, age, budget, dates, available time) into labelled ranges that span the realistic range — e.g. height → "150–160 cm", "160–170 cm", "170–180 cm", "180 cm+". Ask one attribute per question: split "weight and height" into two separate questions when absolutely needed.
returns: Command handled by ClarificationMiddleware: appends the question to ThreadState.clarifications, flips `clarification_pending=true`, and halts Plan Mode for the user's answer. Prefer `write_plan(..., clarifications=[...])` for nonblocking inline plan clarifications.
examples: ask_user_for_clarification(question="How much do you intend to invest?", clarification_type="missing_info", context="The investment-sizing step needs a budget range.", blocks=["todo-sizing", "todo-recommend"], options=[{"label": "Under SGD 50k"}, {"label": "SGD 50k–200k"}, {"label": "Above SGD 200k"}])

ask_user_for_clarification(question="Which region of Indonesia is in scope?", clarification_type="approach_choice", blocks=["todo-region-deepdive", "todo-recommend"], options=[{"label": "Java", "recommended": true}, {"label": "Sumatra"}, {"label": "Eastern Indonesia"}])

ask_user_for_clarification(question="What is your current weight?", clarification_type="missing_info", context="Needed to set a calorie target and a safe weight-loss goal.", blocks=["todo-baseline"], options=[{"label": "60–70 kg"}, {"label": "70–80 kg", "recommended": true}, {"label": "80–90 kg"}, {"label": "90 kg+"}])

ask_user_for_clarification(question="The plan will include deleting 3,200 records in `orders`. Approve before I finalise?", clarification_type="risk_confirmation", context="Effect is irreversible once we leave plan mode.", urgency="blocking")

ask_user_for_clarification(question="Should the plan include updating the README to reflect this rename?", clarification_type="suggestion", context="The README still references the old module name in three places.", blocks=[])

question.description: The clarification question. Be specific, self-contained, and answerable in one short reply. One question per call. End with a question mark.

clarification_type.description: Why clarification is needed. Use `risk_confirmation` whenever the plan will include a destructive action (file deletion, prod writes, force-push, dropping data) — never skip the confirmation step. Use `approach_choice` or `suggestion` when `options` are supplied.

context.description: Optional one-sentence context so the user understands why you're asking (e.g. what you tried, what's ambiguous about the plan, what's at stake). Omit if the question is fully self-explanatory.

options.description: Structured choices the user picks from — each renders as a clickable button (`label` = title, `description` = helper text); mark at most one entry `recommended: true` and the UI highlights it (it is also the choice applied if the user dismisses the question). There is NO free-text input, so options must be exhaustive and directly selectable: for numeric or open-ended attributes give labelled ranges covering the realistic span (e.g. "60–70 kg", "70–80 kg", "80–90 kg", "90 kg+") instead of a prompt to type a value, and never include placeholder options like "Provide stats", "Enter manually", "Skip", or "Other".

label.description: Short option title (1-5 words). Displayed prominently.

description.description: Optional one-line helper text explaining the option, its trade-offs, or its implications for the plan.

recommended.description: If true, the UI marks this option as the suggested choice. At most one option per call should set this to true.

blocks.description: Optional list of todo ids in plan.todos whose readiness depends on this answer. TodoDagMiddleware will keep these todos out of `ready_ids` until the user answers. Pass `[]` (or omit) for questions that don't gate any specific todo.

urgency.description: `deferrable` (default) queues the question and lets the run continue on unblocked todos. `blocking` halts the run immediately until the user answers — use only when no useful planning progress is possible without the answer.

name: present_files
description: Surface one or more workspace files to the user during planning — typically a draft plan document, a research note you've written into the workspace, or a reference artifact the user should review before approving the plan. Only paths under `/mnt/user-data/workspace` may be presented; paths outside that root are rejected with an error. Supported renderable types include PDF, MD, CSV, XLSX, common code/text formats, and images (PNG, JPG, SVG). Do NOT use for files you only need to read for your own analysis — that's what `read_file` is for. In Plan Mode this tool is for keeping the user looped in on planning artifacts, not for declaring task completion. Safe to call in parallel with other tools; an artifact reducer merges concurrent updates without conflict.
returns: Command that appends the normalized virtual paths to `state.artifacts` (deduplicated by the merge reducer) and emits a tool message with the presented paths. The frontend then renders the files in the artifact panel.
examples: present_files(filepaths=["/mnt/user-data/workspace/plan-draft.md"])
present_files(filepaths=["/mnt/user-data/workspace/research-notes.md", "/mnt/user-data/workspace/options-comparison.md"])
present_files(filepaths=["/mnt/user-data/workspace/case-context.md", "/mnt/user-data/workspace/timeline-draft.png"])

filepaths.description: Absolute paths under `/mnt/user-data/workspace`. Paths outside that root return an error. Group related artifacts in a single call rather than calling once per file. Recommended max ~10 paths per call.

name: recall
description: Search long-term memory for facts and context relevant to a short query — especially valuable while planning, where prior decisions, conventions, and user preferences should shape the plan before you ever ask the user. Searches both the workspace scope (per-thread memories) and the global scope (cross-thread / org-wide memories) when both are enabled. Call this BEFORE assuming you have to ask the user for context that may already exist — e.g. the user's role, prior decisions, project or case conventions, regular clients/vendors, dietary or scheduling preferences, preferred tools. Short factual phrases (`"preferred invoice format"`, `"dietary restrictions"`, `"deployment workflow"`) outperform full-sentence questions because the underlying retriever is keyword + embedding based, not conversational. Use cases span software work, legal / administrative case management, and lifestyle lookups. Returns the top-K matches above a relevance threshold; if nothing relevant is stored, you get an explicit 'No relevant memory found.' message and should fall back to asking the user or using sensible defaults.
returns: JSON string `{query, results: [{id, scope, content, category, confidence, score, source}]}` where `score` is the relevance score (higher = more relevant) and `scope` is `workspace` or `global`. Returns the literal string 'No relevant memory found.' when no results clear the threshold, and 'Memory scopes are disabled.' when the memory subsystem is turned off in config.
examples: recall(query="preferred invoice format")
recall(query="client X case notes")
recall(query="dietary restrictions")
recall(query="regular suppliers")
recall(query="preferred python formatter")

query.description: Short factual phrase (2-6 words works best). Avoid full sentences and question words — `"linter preference"` retrieves better than `"What linter does the user prefer?"`.

name: view_image
description: Load an image file into the model's vision channel so you can analyse it visually while drafting a plan — e.g. an architecture diagram the user uploaded, a screenshot of an error, a contract scan, or a chart you need to reference in the plan. This tool requires a vision-capable model and is only exposed when one is in use. Pair with `present_files` when the user should ALSO see the image — `view_image` makes it visible to you, `present_files` makes it visible to them. Do NOT use for non-image files (use `present_files` or `read_file`) or to bulk-display many images (use `present_files` instead). Supported formats: `.jpg`, `.jpeg`, `.png`, `.webp`. Keep images under ~10 MB to avoid token-budget blow-ups.
returns: Command that appends the base64 payload and mime type to `state.viewed_images`, then emits a confirmation tool message. The image is injected into the next model call as a vision input. Returns an error message string if the path is invalid, missing, not absolute, or has an unsupported extension.
examples: view_image(image_path="/mnt/user-data/workspace/uploads/architecture-diagram.png")
view_image(image_path="/mnt/user-data/workspace/uploads/error-screenshot.jpg")
view_image(image_path="/mnt/user-data/workspace/uploads/contract-scan.png")
view_image(image_path="/mnt/user-data/workspace/uploads/event-poster.png")

image_path.description: Absolute path to the image. Workspace virtual paths under `/mnt/user-data/workspace/...` (including `/mnt/user-data/workspace/uploads/...`) are resolved to the thread-scoped physical path. Supported extensions: `.jpg`, `.jpeg`, `.png`, `.webp`. Keep file size under ~10 MB.

name: write_plan
description: Emit the FINISHED execution plan as the canonical plan.md plus the structured plan/todo_graph state the Work agent reads on handoff. This is the terminal action of a plan turn: call it ONCE, after you have investigated enough (with ls/grep/read_file/task/web_search/recall) to scope the work and resolved any blocking questions via ask_user_for_clarification. Provide a title, objective, summary, the domain, and an ordered list of todos. Every todo should carry an observable completion_requirement on its steps; add depends_on ONLY where there is a real data dependency so independent work can run in parallel. Use the optional clarifications list only when a missing detail would fundamentally change the plan's shape — it renders inline in the Execute Plan popup. Do NOT execute any of the plan here; planning produces the document, the Work agent carries it out.
returns: Command that writes `state.plan` (status `draft`, or `approved` under auto mode), `state.todo_graph` (`{nodes, ready_ids}`), the legacy `state.todos` list, `state.plan_history`, and the handoff artifacts; saves `plan.md` + a versioned snapshot under the workspace; emits a `plan_created` SSE event; and flags `plan_just_written` so the planner finalises and ends the turn.
examples: write_plan(title="Netherlands coast trip", objective="A 12-day itinerary along the western Dutch coast and islands.", summary="Day-by-day plan covering transport, lodging, and island hops.", domain="travel", todos=[{"id": "todo-1", "content": "Shortlist coastal towns and islands to visit", "rationale": "Anchors the route before scheduling.", "steps": [{"description": "Identify candidate stops", "completion_requirement": "List has >= 8 towns/islands with region tags"}]}, {"id": "todo-2", "content": "Draft day-by-day itinerary", "depends_on": ["todo-1"], "steps": [{"description": "Sequence stops across 12 days", "completion_requirement": "Itinerary covers 12 days with transport between each stop"}]}])

write_plan(title="Refactor auth module", objective="Replace the session auth with JWT across the API.", summary="Stepwise migration with tests gating each change.", domain="code", todos=[{"id": "todo-1", "content": "Map current auth call sites", "steps": [{"description": "grep for session usage", "completion_requirement": "List of files+lines touching session auth"}]}, {"id": "todo-2", "content": "Implement JWT issuance", "depends_on": ["todo-1"]}], clarifications=[{"question": "Token lifetime?", "options": [{"label": "15 min + refresh", "recommended": True}, {"label": "24h"}]}])

objective.description: One-paragraph description of the intended end state the user wants.

summary.description: 1-2 sentence overview of what the plan will accomplish.

domain.description: Dominant domain — one of code|research|legal|life_admin|data|shopping|food|events|travel|learning|generic. Shapes dependency defaults.

todos.description: Ordered execution todos. Use ids 'todo-1', 'todo-2', ... Add depends_on only for real data dependencies; keep independent todos unsequenced so they can run in parallel.

rationale.description: Why this step exists and why now (1 sentence).

depends_on.description: Ids of todos that MUST complete before this one starts. Omit for independent work.

subagent_type.description: Suggested subagent for the Work agent to use, when owner is 'subagent'.

objective.description: Phase-level outcome this todo achieves (1-2 sentences).

failure_fallback.description: What the Work agent should do if a step fails (e.g. best-effort labelled, or ask_user_for_clarification).

completion_requirement.description: Optional todo-level observable check, when it adds something beyond the final step.

steps.description: Ordered execution steps; each MUST have an observable completion_requirement.

description.description: Short action sentence for the step.

completion_requirement.description: Concrete check proving the step is done (e.g. 'file has >= 10 entries').

subagent_types.description: Suggested subagents for this step (Work-agent hint).

tools.description: Suggested tools for this step (Work-agent hint).

output_artifact_path.description: Virtual path under /mnt/user-data/workspace, only when the completion_requirement references a file.

constraints.description: Hard constraints the work must respect.

acceptance_criteria.description: Observable success criteria for the whole plan.

clarifications.description: Optional clarifying questions surfaced in the Execute Plan popup. Use only when a missing detail would fundamentally change the plan.

options.description: 2-4 options; mark exactly one recommended:true and list it first.

name: ls
description: List the contents of a directory in tree format, up to 2 levels deep — useful in Plan Mode for surveying the codebase, an uploads folder, or a case-file directory before deciding the plan's shape. Faster and more readable than `bash("ls -la ...")` for quick discovery — prefer this when you just want to see what's in a directory. Returns each child on its own line with simple indentation for the second level. Does NOT honor `.gitignore` — hidden and ignored files appear in the listing. For deeper recursion, exact-name file search, or filtering, defer that to execution (Work Mode) rather than expanding the plan's scope here. Empty directories return the literal string `(empty)`.
returns: Newline-joined tree of children (max 2 levels). Returns the literal `(empty)` for empty directories, `Error: Directory not found: <path>` if the path does not exist, `Error: Permission denied: <path>` if unreadable, or `Error: ...` on other sandbox failures.
examples: ls(description="Survey uploads before planning intake", path="/mnt/user-data/workspace/uploads")
ls(description="Survey repo root before scoping the plan", path="/mnt/user-data/workspace")

description.description: Short one-line explanation of why you're listing this directory. Shown in the activity timeline. Pass FIRST in the call.

path.description: Absolute path to the directory to list. Virtual `/mnt/user-data/...` paths are translated automatically.

name: read_file
description: Read the contents of a text file — source code, configuration, logs, markdown, a contract draft, a case note — to inform the plan. In Plan Mode you should read before you decide: if your plan depends on what's in a file, read it now rather than guessing. Supply `start_line` and `end_line` TOGETHER (both or neither) to read a 1-indexed inclusive range; omit both to read the whole file. Prefer reading a specific range for large files — full reads are wasteful for files over a few hundred lines. Do NOT use this tool for binary files or images — use `view_image` for images, and defer unknown binary inspection to execution.
returns: String containing the file contents (or the requested line slice). Returns the literal `(empty)` for empty files, `Error: File not found: <path>` if missing, `Error: Path is a directory, not a file: <path>` if you point at a directory, `Error: Permission denied reading file: <path>` if unreadable, or `Error: ...` on other failures.
examples: read_file(description="Read entrypoint before scoping the plan", path="/mnt/user-data/workspace/main.py")
read_file(description="Review contract obligations to scope the plan", path="/mnt/user-data/workspace/uploads/contract-acme-v3.md")
read_file(description="Inspect logs around the crash before planning a fix", path="/mnt/user-data/workspace/run.log", start_line=100, end_line=160)
read_file(description="Read invoice line items before planning the rewrite", path="/mnt/user-data/workspace/invoice-2026-Q2-acme.csv")

description.description: Short one-line explanation of why you're reading this file. Shown in the activity timeline. Pass FIRST in the call.

path.description: Absolute path to the file to read. Virtual `/mnt/user-data/...` paths are translated automatically. Text files only — use `view_image` for images.

start_line.description: Optional 1-indexed inclusive start line. Must be supplied TOGETHER with `end_line`. If only one of the pair is provided, the range is ignored and the whole file is returned.

end_line.description: Optional 1-indexed inclusive end line. Must be supplied TOGETHER with `start_line`. If `end_line` exceeds the file length, you get the lines up to the actual end with no error.

name: grep
description: Search file contents for a regular-expression pattern, recursively — in Plan Mode this is your primary way to MAP and UNDERSTAND a codebase or document folder before drafting the plan: find where a symbol, route, config key, or phrase lives, trace how the pieces connect, and gauge the size and shape of the work. Skips binary files and common noise directories (.git, node_modules, .venv, __pycache__, build/cache). Each match is returned as `path:line:text`. Read-only and safe for scope discovery; does NOT honor `.gitignore`. Pair with `ls` (structure) and `read_file` (read the specific lines a match points at) to build an accurate picture of the repo.
returns: Newline-joined matches as `path:line:text` (capped at `max_results`, default 200). Returns the literal `(no matches)` when nothing matches, an appended truncation note when the cap is hit, or `Error: ...` on sandbox failure.
examples: grep(description="Find the app entrypoint", pattern="def main", path="/mnt/user-data/workspace", include="*.py")
grep(description="Locate route registration to map the API", pattern="include_router|add_route", path="/mnt/user-data/workspace")
grep(description="Survey TODO/FIXME markers to scope work", pattern="TODO|FIXME", path="/mnt/user-data/workspace", case_insensitive=true)

description.description: Short one-line explanation of why you're searching. Shown in the activity timeline. Pass FIRST in the call.

pattern.description: Regular-expression pattern to search for (basic POSIX regex, as understood by `grep`). Pass SECOND in the call.

path.description: Absolute path to the file or directory to search. Virtual `/mnt/user-data/...` paths are translated automatically. Pass THIRD in the call.

include.description: Optional filename glob to restrict the search (e.g. `*.py`, `*.md`). Omit to search all files.

max_results.description: Maximum number of matching lines to return (1-1000). Default 200; output past the cap is truncated with a note.

name: task
description: Delegate ONE scoped scope-discovery objective to a planning subagent that runs in its own isolated context, then returns a structured brief you reason over while drafting the plan. In Plan Mode the only subagents available are read-only planning helpers — they investigate and report, they NEVER execute work or produce the deliverable. Use this to understand the problem faster, especially by running several finders in PARALLEL across independent facets (e.g. one `finder-agent` mapping the backend while another maps the frontend, or several `scope-researcher` calls covering different sub-topics). The subagent does NOT see this conversation — its `prompt` must be fully self-contained: state the one facet/area, any constraints, and the brief you want back. Keep each delegation to a single narrow objective; split broad asks into multiple `task` calls. Do NOT use for: work you can do directly with `ls`/`grep`/`read_file`/`web_search`; anything that edits files or executes the plan (that is Work Mode); questions that need the user (use `ask_user_for_clarification`).
returns: String containing the planning subagent's structured brief (truncated if it exceeds ~20k tokens). A dispatched task failure returns an error ToolMessage with structured terminal metadata. Lifecycle events (`task_started`, `task_running`, `task_completed`, `task_failed`, `task_timed_out`) stream out-of-band to the client.
examples: task(description="Map the repo structure", prompt="Map and understand the backend at /mnt/user-data/workspace. Read the README and entrypoints (main.py/app.py), then report: top-level structure, where the HTTP layer / business logic / config live, how startup wires things together, and any constraints worth knowing before planning improvements. Do not propose changes.", subagent_type="finder-agent")

task(description="Scope the comparison", prompt="Understand the SCOPE of comparing managed Postgres providers for a small SaaS. Return: the sub-dimensions that matter (pricing model, regions, backups, connection limits, etc.), credible source types to consult during execution, and any ambiguities the planner should resolve. Do not pick a winner.", subagent_type="scope-researcher")

task(description="Understand the auth module", prompt="Map /mnt/user-data/workspace/src/auth. Report how authentication flows (entrypoints, middleware, token handling), where each responsibility lives, and what a change to the login path would touch. Read-only; no edits.", subagent_type="finder-agent")

description.description: Short (3-5 word) label for UI and log display, e.g. `"Map the repo structure"`. Shown verbatim in the activity timeline.

prompt.description: The full, self-contained objective for the planning subagent. Must include: (1) the one facet/area to investigate, (2) any scope limits, (3) the brief you want back. The subagent does not see this conversation. Never ask it to produce the deliverable — only to investigate and report.

subagent_type.description: Which planning subagent to invoke. `scope-researcher`: understands the SCOPE of one facet using `web_search` + `query_knowledge_vault` (sub-topics, sources, ambiguities). `finder-agent`: FINDS AND UNDERSTANDS local files/directories using `grep`/`ls`/`read_file` (maps a repo/folder, reads README/entrypoints/configs, reports where things live). Subagent graph-step budgets come only from config.yaml.
